front: Work: Jean Valjean steals this clergyman's silverware, but this clergyman covers for him and gives him two candlesticks
Work: in this novel, a jilted factory worker is dismissed and forced to sell her hair and teeth to support her daughter
Work: in this novel, a street urchin shows two other urchins (secretly his brothers) where he lives inside the Elephant of the Bastille
Work: a digression in this novel explains the Battle of Waterloo with a capital letter placed over the ground in a chapter titled 'A'
Work: a digression in this novel concerns the Paris sewers, whose history is laid out in a section called 'The Intestine of the Leviathan'
Work: in this novel, Enjolras leads the revolutionary group the Friends of the ABC
Work: in this novel, a character becomes a factory owner under the false name Monsieur Madeleine
Work: in this novel, the protagonist carries Marius through the sewers during a revolution
Work: Inspector Javert is the son of a convict father and fortune teller mother and is self-conscious about being born in prison
Work: in this novel, the Thenardiers stage a robbery at Gorbeau House with the Patron-Minette gang
Work: this novel includes an interlude set in 1830 listing 'Algeria too harshly conquered' among political grievances
Work: the gypsy Esmeralda owns a trained goat named Djali

front: Work: the speaker of this poem says 'the day for me will be as the night' and walks 'alone, unknown, my back curved, my hands crossed'

front: Work: the speaker of this poem resolves to place 'a bouquet of green holly and flowering heather' on a grave

front: Work: the fourth of six books in this poetry collection is titled Pauca Meae, from a phrase in Virgil's Eclogues; divided into 'Once' and 'Today'
Work: the poem 'In Villequier' implores God to 'Let me cry!' and is named for the city where this poet's daughter drowned in the Seine
Work: this author popularized the three-part alexandrine, or alexandrin ternaire
Work: riots between Romantics and Classicists at performances of this play are called the 'Battle of' its name
Work: in this play, Charles V (Don Carlos), Don Ruy Gomez, and John of Aragon (disguised as the title bandit) all love Dona Sol
Work: supporters dressed in outrageous costumes to foil censors at the premiere of this play
Work: the preface to this play attacks the 'worm-eaten timber' of the classical unities and calls for drama allowing 'grotesque with the sublime'
Work: the preface to this play calls the stage an 'optical point' where all humanity should be reflected by the 'wand of art'
Work: this was Hugo's first play but was never staged, having over 400 pages in iambic pentameter spoken by 77 characters
Work: in this novel, the fisherman Gilliatt fights an octopus underwater; the novel popularized the French word 'pieuvre'

front: Work: this novel ends with the protagonist sitting in the Gild-Holm-'Ur seat watching the Cashmere sail away carrying Ebenezer Caudray and Deruchette

front: Work: in this novel, Sieur Clubin tries to abscond with his employer's money and drowns when attacked by a large octopus

front: Work: this author's last novel describes the fictional Marquis de Lantenac's revolt against the Republic during the Chouannerie

Work: this 1829 short novel is written from the perspective of a man about to be executed at the guillotine

front: Work: the disfigured Gwynplaine travels with the carnival vendor Ursus and the wolf Homo in this novel
Work: this author coined the term 'Comprachico' for a group that 'worked on man as the Chinese worked on trees'
Work: in this play, an indentured servant is disguised as a nobleman for a practical joke, becomes prime minister, and becomes the Queen's lover
Work: in this novel, the narrator is captured by maroon general Biassou during the Haitian Revolution but is saved by the title slave leader
Work: this unfinished religious epic details events from the fall of Satan to the storming of the Bastille, with sections on Nimrod and the death of Jesus Christ
Author: this author spent 15 years exiled on the island of Guernsey for criticisms of Napoleon III's government
Author: critic Sainte-Beuve's early infatuation with this author morphed into an affair with his wife Adele Foucher
Author: Alan Spitzer placed this young author in the French Generation of 1820 alongside Lamartine and the Comte de Vigny
Author: this author's daughter Adele suffered from schizophrenia, obsessed over British officer Albert Pinson, and sometimes gives her name to Love Fever Syndrome
Author: this author told abolitionist Victor Schoelcher 'God offers Africa to Europe. Take it.'
Author: this author created over 4,000 drawings, some anticipating Surrealist automatic writing; one depicts an octopus spelling his initials
Author: this author is worshipped by Cao Dai; an image in the Tay Ninh temple shows him signing a covenant with God alongside Sun Yat-sen
Author: after publishing his first poems at age 22, this writer received a royal pension
Author: this author was best known as a poet in France during his lifetime, but today is known for his novels
